key_points: A circle is the set of all points in a plane at a fixed distance (radius) from a fixed point (center).
Radius: Distance from center to any point on the circle.
Chord: Line segment joining two points on the circle.
Tangent: Line touching the circle at exactly one point.
Secant: Line intersecting the circle at two points.
The perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord.
Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center.
formulas: Length of arc: L = (θ/360) × 2πr, where θ is in degrees
Chord length: l = 2√(r² - d²), where d is distance from center to chord
examples: Radius of a circle with circumference 31.4 cm → r = C/2π = 31.4/6.28 = 5 cm
Area of circle with r = 7 cm → A = πr² = 3.14×49 ≈ 153.86 cm²
Chord of length 8 cm at distance 3 cm from center, r = √(d² + (l/2)²) = √(3² + 4²) = 5 cm
Length of arc of 60° in circle of radius 6 cm → L = (60/360)×2π6 = 2π ≈ 12.56 cm
exam_tips: Always identify radius, diameter, chord, tangent in diagrams.
Use perpendicular from center to chord to find distance or length of chord.
Convert angles to radians if formula requires.
Check if the problem involves arc, sector, or segment and use correct formula.
For tangent problems, remember radius ⊥ tangent at the point of contact.
